metadata.title: Why Fair Credit-Splitting Has Exactly One Answer

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. And is there only one right answer? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: And is there only one right answer??
props.segment: Why Fair Credit-Splitting Has Exactly One

beats.narration_text: Why fair credit-splitting has exactly one answer.

card.copy:
Why Fair Credit-Splitting
Has Exactly One Answer

beats.narration_text: A credit model has three inputs — income, debt ratio, and zip code. Together they push the probability of approval from 55 percent to 72 percent. That's 17 percentage points to distribute. How much credit does each feature deserve?
If features interact — if income matters more when debt is low — how do you divide credit fairly? And is there only one right answer?

props.headline: If features interact — if income matters more when debt is…

card.copy: If features interact, how do you divide credit fairly — and is there only one right answer?

beats.narration_text: The order you add features changes how much each one contributes. Add income first and it shifts the prediction a lot. Add it last and it shifts less, because debt already explained some of it. So which contribution do you count?
Count all of them. For three features, there are six possible orderings. For each ordering, compute what income adds when it arrives. Average those six numbers. That average is income's fair share.
Imagine the features entering a room in a random order.

props.headline: Imagine the features entering a room in a random order.

beats.image_description: An open doorway viewed from inside a room, with soft light coming through, suggesting someone about to enter

beats.narration_text: Each time a feature enters, it finds a different coalition already there and adds what it can on top. Income enters an empty room and adds 8 points. Debt enters next and adds 6 more. Zip code arrives last and adds the remaining 3.

Average those marginal contributions across all orderings — all six arrangements for three features — and you get the Shapley value: the unique fair share for each input. Income gets 0.073, debt 0.062, zip code 0.035. They sum exactly to 0.17.

Think of the features entering a room in a random order. Every ordering is equally likely. When a feature enters, it finds some coalition already there. The Shapley value is the average change in the prediction across all those random arrivals.

The answer is unique because it satisfies four axioms. Efficiency: credits sum to the full prediction. Symmetry: identical features get identical credit. Dummy: unused features get zero. Additivity: credits from separate models combine cleanly. No other attribution rule satisfies all four.

The attribution to zip code is a real number describing the model's behavior, and it tells you nothing about the world. It does not say whether zip code is a proxy for race. It does not say whether the effect would persist if an applicant moved.

A recommendation model pushes article probability from 45 to 68 percent — 23 points from three signals: reading time, scroll depth, and headline clicks. A naive attribution adds reading time first and credits it 0.17. But add it last and it gets 0.09 — the value shifts with the order. Shapley averages all six orderings. Reading time's fair share: 0.14. Scroll depth: 0.06. Clicks: 0.03. They sum exactly to 0.23. That sum is guaranteed — it is the efficiency axiom working.
Shapley values give each feature its uniquely fair share — averaged over all the orders it could have arrived. The math is exact. The model is not the world.

props.headline: Shapley values give each feature its uniquely fair share — averaged…

card.copy: Shapley values give each feature its uniquely fair share.
card.sub: The math is exact. The model is not the world.
